coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots with high quantum yield prepared by heterogeneous nucleation processes
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Chun-Yao
Venkatesan, Shanmuganathan
Herman, Andy
Wang, Chi-Lo
Teng, Hsisheng
Lee, Yuh-Lang
coredata.dc:description: Homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation processes are employed to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CDs) by a solvothermal method, with o-phenylenediamine used as the precursor. For the heterogeneous process, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles were employed as the nucleation seeds. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs prepared by homogeneous (CDs) and TiO2-induced heterogeneous (T-CDs) methods are compared herein. The experimental results show that, in comparison to the CDs, the n-π * transition peak in the UV–vis absorbance spectra of T-CDs red-shifts slightly and becomes broad, implying smaller and multiple band gaps of the T-CDs. The PL spectra demonstrate that the emission wavelengths of CDs and T-CDs are about 530 and 496 nm, respectively, which are independent of the reaction conditions. By tuning the operation conditions, the maximum quantum yield (QY) obtained by the CDs is 18 %, while a value of 68 % can be accomplished by the T-CDs. If N-doped TiO2 nanoparticles were utilized as the nucleation seeds, an even higher QY (85.9 %) could be achieved. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis demonstrated that the T-CDs contain a higher percentage of N atoms and a lower fraction of pyrrolic state N, which is inferred to be the reasons causing the shorter emission wavelength and higher QYs of the T-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heterogeneous nucleation
Nucleation seed
Titanium dioxide
Photoluminescence
Quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photo luminance images of the QDs in ethanol solution
Model proposed for the growth of CDs on the TiO2 surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The light absorption (a) and PL(b) spectra of the CDs prepared by homogeneous (CD) and heterogeneous (T-CQ) processes at 180 °C for 12 h. The weight ratio of TiO2 to oPD is 1.6.
The light absorption and PL spectra of the T-CDs prepared by heterogeneous processes at various temperatures for 12 h. The weight ratio of TiO2 to oPD is 1.6.

The UV–vis absorbance (a) and PL (b) spectra measured for T-CD prepared using 25 nm and 100 nm TiO2 particles. The TiO2/oPD ratios are 0.1 and 1.6, respectively, for 25 nm and 100 nm particles. The reaction was performed at 120 °C for 12 h.
The optical images of CD (a,d), T-CD (b,e) and N-T-CD (c,f) dispersed in ethanol solution under daylight (a, b, c) and UV light (365 nm) illumination (d, e, f).
TEM images of CD (a), T-CD (b) and N-T-CD and the lattice structure of the CD (e), T-CD (f) and N-T-CD. Particle size distribution diagrams of CD (g), T-CD (h) and N-T-CD (i).
IR Spectra of the (a) CD, (b) T-CD and N-T-CD.
The de-convoluted C, N and O peaks of XPS spectra for CD (a-c), T-CD (d-f) and N-T-CD (g-i).
The photoluminescence properties of CD and T-CDs at various temperature. The weight ratio of TiO2 to oPD was 1.6.
The photoluminescence properties of T-CDs prepared using reaction time. The temperature and TiO2/oPD weight ratio were controlled at 120 °C and 1.6, respectively.
The photoluminescence properties of T-CDs prepared using different particle sizes and surface modified TiO2. The reactions were controlled at 120 °C for 12 h.

The element percentages of C, N and O calculated from the XPS spectra. The percentages of different carbon and nitrogen states calculated from the de-convoluted C1s and N1s calculated from the XPS spectra of the three CDs. The reaction was performed at 120 °C for 12 h and the TiO2/oPD and N-TiO2/oPD ratio controlled at 1.6.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots with high quantum yield prepared by heterogeneous nucleation processes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, National Cheng Kung University, No. 1, University Road, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, National Cheng Kung University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, No. 1, University Road, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering,National Cheng Kung University, No. 1, University Road, Tainan 70101, Taiwan. Fax: +886-6-2344496

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering,National Cheng Kung University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation processes are employed to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CDs) by a solvothermal method, with o-phenylenediamine used as the precursor. For the heterogeneous process, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles were employed as the nucleation seeds. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs prepared by homogeneous (CDs) and TiO2-induced heterogeneous (T-CDs) methods are compared herein. The experimental results show that, in comparison to the CDs, the n-π * transition peak in the UV–vis absorbance spectra of T-CDs red-shifts slightly and becomes broad, implying smaller and multiple band gaps of the T-CDs. The PL spectra demonstrate that the emission wavelengths of CDs and T-CDs are about 530 and 496 nm, respectively, which are independent of the reaction conditions. By tuning the operation conditions, the maximum quantum yield (QY) obtained by the CDs is 18 %, while a value of 68 % can be accomplished by the T-CDs. If N-doped TiO2 nanoparticles were utilized as the nucleation seeds, an even higher QY (85.9 %) could be achieved. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis demonstrated that the T-CDs contain a higher percentage of N atoms and a lower fraction of pyrrolic state N, which is inferred to be the reasons causing the shorter emission wavelength and higher QYs of the T-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are commonly referred to as semiconductor nanoparticles with size regimes between 1 and 10 nm [1]. These ultrasmall semiconductor particles possess an ability to confine electrons, termed “quantum confinement”. The quantum confinement effect causes a splitting of energy levels both in the conduction and valence bands of the QDs, as well as a size-dependent bandgap [2]. When the QDs are illuminated by a photon of sufficient energy, an electron will be excited, moving from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an electron-hole pair known as an “exciton” [3]. When the exciton recombines, its energy can be released as fluorescence; this effect triggers specific optical and electronic properties differing from their corresponding bulk material, leading to a variety of applications in photovoltaic devices, LEDs, displays, fluorescent probes, photodetectors, photocatalysts, imaging and biosensing [4–9]. Moreover, the features of QDs can be easily tuned by controlling their size and composition [10].

Typical semiconductor QDs are usually made of heavy metal elements. Compounds such as CdS, CdSe, CdTe, PbS, PbSe, CdSeS and InAs are commonly studied in the relevant literature [11,12]. Some of these semiconductor QDs have shown excellent fluorescence properties. However, the heavy metals in these QDs are toxic; as a result, there is now a restriction or ban on the utilization of these elements [13]. Considering the pollution of the environment, eco-friendly QDs were subsequently developed [14].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have received considerable attention in the past several years as a suitable substitute for semiconductor QDs due to their low cost, high photoluminescence (PL), stability and biocompatibility [15–23]. Owing to these inherent characteristics, CDs and their composites have continued to be utilized in a variety of applications, such as photocatalysis [15,16], bioimaging [17], fluorescence sensing [18], cancer therapy [19], light emitting devices [20], solar concentrators [21], solar cells [22] and battery technologies [21–23] etc. However, the synthesis of CDs with high quantum yields (QYs) and tunable luminescence is still considered important for various applications [17]. It is well known that the PL spectra and the QYs of the CDs were generally influenced by several parameters, such as the precursors and their concentrations, synthesis temperature and time, and doping elements [24] Accordingly, the CDs with different characteristics were prepared using different precursors [25–27] derived by various synthesis methods [28]: hydrothermal [29–31], solvothermal [17,20,32–36] ultrasonic [37], electrochemical [38], solid-state reaction [39] and microwave assisted method [40,41]. Among these methods, the solvothermal method is the most popular one for preparing CDs since it is a cost-effective, low-temperature, and ecofriendly method [17,20,32–36]. Also, the solvothermal method enables the preparation of CDs with different surface states, uniform size and high QY. For example, Zhu et al. utilized m-phenylenediamine dissolved in ethanol and hydrochloric acid to prepare green CDs with an average size of 5 nm and a QY of 58.65 % [42]. Following their study, orange emissive CDs were prepared using 1-amono-2-napthol hydrochloride and citric acid dissolved in ethanol [43]. These CDs showed an average size of ∼2.5 nm with a QY of 7 % [38]. Very recently, full-color CDs (∼1.71–2.42 nm) with QYs of 72 % were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and various weak acid reagents [33]. In another study, Cl and N co-doped CDs were prepared using oPD dissolved in chloroform [20]. These monodispersed CDs with mean sizes of 2.2–3.02 nm showed high quantum yields of 33.7–88.9 %. Pontes et al., also prepared full color CDs with dimensions between 2 and 10 nm and a QY of 15 % using citric acid and urea in dimethylformamide [36].

Usually, the synthesis of CDs is performed using homogeneous nucleation and growth processes [44,45]. In the presence of nucleation seeds, the reaction will be performed via a heterogeneous process. For both processes, supersaturation is required to overcome the nucleation energy barrier, thereby initiating the reaction. In the initial stage of the nucleation, the nuclei size and Gibbs free energy (△G) increase with the increased supersaturation. When the sizes of the embryos are smaller than the critical value (r * ), they are unstable and the reaction is unable to proceed. As the supersaturation increases to a critical value, the nuclei grow to a critical size and the Gibb free energy approaches the maxima value (△G*), which is the energy barrier of this reaction. Comparing the homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, the homogeneous reaction has a higher △G* than that of the heterogeneous one; therefore, a higher supersaturation is required to initiate the reaction. On the other hand, the energy barrier can be overcome easily in the presence of nucleation seeds [46,47].

In the extant literature, the synthesis of CDs was usually carried out using a homogeneous nucleation reaction [48]; the studies regarding the heterogeneous system were relatively fewer [49,50]. In this study, a heterogeneous nucleation and growth process is proposed to synthesize CDs using oPD as the precursors. TiO2 nanoparticles were utilized as the seeds in a solvothermal reaction. The properties of CDs fabricated by the heterogeneous process were compared to those using the homogeneous one. In addition, the effects of various reaction parameters on the properties of CDs were also studied.

Ammonium hydrogen carbonate (NH4HCO3) was used as a regent to prepare N-doped TiO2 particles according to the method reported in the literature [51,52]. First of all, 3 g TiO2 and 8.46 g NH4HCO3 were added into 50 ml of deionized water, followed by stirring homogenization at room temperature for 1 h. The solution was then heated at 100 °C for 24 h in a reflux reactor. Finally, the TiO2 powders were separated from the solution by centrifugation and heated in a 100 °C oven to evaporate the residue solvent.

CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method using ethanol as the solvent. First of all, 108 mg of oPD were dissolved in 10 ml of ethanol; the solution was then transferred to a high-pressure Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The synthesis was carried out at various temperatures and reaction times. After the solvothermal reaction, the solution containing CDs was subjected to silica column chromatography using ethyl acetate as the eluent, thereby obtaining a purified CD solution. The eluent solvent was then evaporated using a rotavapor to obtain solid CDs. Afterwards, the solid CDs were re-dispersed in ethanol for characterization. For the heterogeneous synthesis process, various amounts of TiO2 nanoparticles were introduced into the solution. After the heterogeneous reaction, the TiO2 nanoparticles were separated from the solution by centrifugation before performing column chromatography.

First of all, the synthesis of CDs was carried out by a homogeneous system at 180 °C for 12 h. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of the CDs in ethanol solution and the precursor (oPD) are shown in Fig. 1(a). For the CD spectra, two apparent peaks at λ = 259 and λ = 426 nm were observed, corresponding to the π - π * and n - π * transition, respectively, indicating the formation of CD. The PL spectra of the CD at different excitation wavelengths were measured and are shown in Fig. S1. It was found that the wavelengths around the π - π * transition did not generate fluorescence, and apparent emitting peaks were excited by the light in the region of n - π * transition. Furthermore, the peaks of emitting spectra were located around 532 nm, independent of the wavelengths of the illumination light. It was also found that the highest PL intensity was excited by light of 420 nm in wavelength; the corresponding PL spectra are shown in Fig. 1(b). The highest quantum yield (QY) and the FWHM measured for this CD were 15.7 % and 73.2 nm, respectively ( Table 1). These values are similar to those reported in the literature for CDs prepared using the same system [53].

To study the effects of nanoparticles on the nucleation and growth of CDs, TiO2 nanoparticles with a diameter of 25 nm were introduced into the precursor solution. First of all, a weight ratio of TiO2 to oPD (TiO2/oPD) was controlled at 1.6. The CDs synthesized in the presence of TiO2 are termed T-CDs hereafter. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of the T-CD is also shown in Fig. 1(a) for comparison. It demonstrates that the spectrum of T-CD slightly differs from that of the CD. The peak of π - π * transition does not shift, but that of the n - π * transition red-shifted slightly and became discrete, consisting of two sub-peaks. The PL spectrum of the T-CD is also shown in Fig. 1(b). It shows that the emitting light blue-shifts to ≈ 496 nm in comparison to that of the CDs. It was found that the emitting wavelengths are independent of the excitation wavelengths and the highest PL intensity was excited by the 420 nm light, which matches the properties of CDs. Surprisingly, the QY of the T-CDs increased significantly to 63.9 % (Table 1), a value much higher than that of the CDs.

Operation temperature is an important parameter affecting a reaction mechanism and the properties of the products [54]. Therefore, various temperatures (120, 180 and 200 °C) were utilized to synthesize the T-CDs, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 2. It shows that the UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra are nearly identical for various reaction temperatures. Table 1 exhibits the wavelengths of the emitting light and the corresponding QYs excited by 420 nm light. It shows that for all the reaction temperatures studied here, the wavelengths of emitting light are nearly identical (ca. 496 nm), and QY over 59 % can be obtained. It is interesting to find that decreasing the reaction temperature to 120 °C can achieve a QY (68.7 %) higher than that obtained by 180 °C. This result implies that with the presence of TiO2 seeds, the energy barrier of the reaction decreases; therefore, the reaction can also be performed at lower temperatures. For comparison, the temperature effect on the properties of CDs synthesized in the homogeneous system were also studied, and the results are shown in Table 1. It demonstrates that the emitting wavelengths are nearly identical (530 nm), and the QYs decrease slightly with decreasing temperature from 17.8 % (200 °C) to 10.77 % (120 °C). Based on these results, a lower temperature is not favored for the reaction in a homogeneous system.

Another parameter affecting the properties of QDs is the reaction time [55]. To study this effect, the synthesis was performed at 120 °C for 4, 8, and 12 h. The UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra measured for the T-CD are shown in Fig. S2, and the related parameters are listed in Table 2, demonstrating that the UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra did not change significantly by altering the reaction time. Besides, the emitting wavelengths are identical (ca. 496 nm). It was also found that the QYs decreased slightly with a decrease in reaction time and a value of 61.9 % could still be achieved after a 4-h reaction.

Although the results obtained above indicate that the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles can affect the properties of CDs, the effects of TiO2 concentration remain unclear. To study this issue, various amounts of TiO2 were introduced into the autoclave at a reaction temperature of 120 °C for 12 h. Fig. S3 shows that the UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra are nearly identical for TiO2/oPD ratios between 0.1 and 2.1. Regarding the PL properties shown in Table 2, the emitting wavelengths are independent of the ratios studied here (ca. 496 nm); besides, all the cases have very similar QYs except for the ratio of 0.1, which has a slightly smaller value of 57.2 %. These results demonstrate that a small amount of TiO2 is sufficient to induce the heterogeneous nucleation process of CDs.

The TiO2 particles utilized above have a size of 25 nm. To study the size effect of the particles, 100 nm TiO2 particles were employed as the nucleation seeds. TiO2/oPD weight ratios of 0.1 and 1.6 were utilized for particles of 25 nm and 100 nm, respectively, and the synthesis was controlled at 120 °C for 12 h. The UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra of T-CDs prepared using the two particles are demonstrated in Fig. 3. It appears that the UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra obtained for 100 nm particles differ from those of 25 nm ones, but are highly similar to those of the CD prepared by a homogeneous reaction system. The emitting wavelength and QYs measured for the T-CDs prepared by 100 nm particles are 533 nm and 13.0 %, respectively (shown in Table 3). According to these results, the 100 nm TiO2 particles cannot act as seeds to induce the heterogeneous nucleation reaction. Since the overall surface area of the 100 nm particles with TiO2/oPD ratio of 1.6 is the same as that of the 25 nm particles with ratio of 0.1, the different QYs (13.0 % vs. 57.2 %) of the two systems were not caused by the difference in surface area but is inferred to be the curvature effects of the two particles.

Since the TiO2 particles act as the nucleation seed in the synthesis process, the interaction between TiO2 surface and oPD molecules should be an important factor in determining the properties of the CDs. Therefore, N-doped TiO2 particles were utilized to modify the TiO2-oPD interaction in the heterogeneous reaction, in aiming to improve the performance of the CDs. TiO2 particles of 25 nm were treated by NH4HCO3 to prepare N-doped TiO2 (termed as N-TiO2). The XPS analysis exhibited that after the N-doping process, the element compositions of Ti, O and N were 35.4 %, 63.1 % and 1.5 %, which differ from the 31.5 %, 68.5 % and 0 %, respectively, of the bare TiO2. The presence of the N element indicates the achievement of doping process ( Table 4).

N-TiO2 nanoparticles with an N-TiO2/oPD ratio of 1.6 were utilized in the synthesis process and the reaction was carried out at 120 °C for 12 h. The CDs prepared were termed N-T-CDs. The UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra measured for the N-T-CDs did not show a significant difference (Fig. S4); however, the QY increased significantly to 85.9 % (Table 3). In this study, the surface modifying agent (NH4HCO3) and bare TiO2 particles were utilized simultaneously as additives in the reaction to evaluate the effect of NH4HCO3. It was found that the UV–vis, PL spectra and emitting wavelength are similar to those of the systems using TiO2 nanoparticles, but that the QY decreases greatly to 7.7 % (Table 3). This result indicates that the increase of QY of N-T-CDs was not caused by the NH4HCO3 in the system; furthermore, the presence of NH4HCO3 in the precursor solution is unfavorable to the QYs. On the basis of these results, the importance of the seed-precursor interaction on the properties of the CDs is confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo luminance images of the QDs in ethanol solution
ce:section.ce:para: The optical images of the CD, T-CD and N-T-CD dispersed in ethanol solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm) illumination are shown in Fig. 4. All the solutions display transparent and light-yellow color under daylight (Fig. 4(a–c)). By illumination of UV light, yellow-green fluorescent light exhibits for CDs, but both T-CDs and N-T-CDs show a green fluorescence. Furthermore, the intensities of the fluorescent light are N-T-CD>T-CD>CD. These results are consistent with the emitting wavelength and QYs measured from these CDs.

The results reported above indicate that the CDs synthesized in the presence of TiO2 nanoparticles may trigger different optical properties in comparison to those without TiO2. To figure out the role of TiO2 particles in the synthesis process, as well as their effects on the properties of CDs, the CDs were subjected to the TEM, FTIR and XPS analyses.

The TEM images shown in Fig. 5 demonstrate that these carbon dots were well dispersed in the ethanol solution, implying the presence of polar functional groups on the surfaces. The mean size of CDs, T-CDs and N-T-CDs are 3.09, 3.79 and 3.23 nm, respectively, and those of the lattice space are 0.227 nm, 0.262 nm and 0.218. It was reported that larger QDs usually lead to a red shift of the PL emission [33,36]. However, this tendency was not found in this work, and both the particle sizes and lattice spaces did not show meaningful difference. Therefore, the different properties of the three CDs cannot be ascribed to the sizes of the QDs.

The FTIR spectra of the three QDs shown in Fig. 6 indicate that the QDs consist of many functional groups. In general, the spectra of T-CD and N-T-CD are very similar, but slightly differ from that of CDs. For the CDs, a broad peak corresponding to the O-H group appears around 3200–3700 cm−1. However, for T-CDs and N-T-CDs, this peak shifted to 2800–3250 cm−1, assigned to the N-H group. In the region of small wavenumbers, the peaks are more complicated. A close inspection reveals a slight difference. Functional groups of C-N (1110), CC (1375, 1510) and CO (1630) can be observed on the CDs. For the T-CDs and N-T-CDs, peaks corresponding to C-O-C (1225), CC (1375), C-N (1450), and CO (1630) group appear. These results demonstrate that various polar functional groups were present on the surface of the three CDs, allowing them to be dispersed in polar solvents such as ethanol and water. Besides, the slight difference in the FTIR spectra indicate the effect of the synthesize methods on the surface properties of the CDs.

The survey scan spectra of XPS analysis measured for the three CDs are shown in Fig. S5, which demonstrates apparent peaks of C, N and O. The atomic percentages of the 3 elements calculated from the spectra are listed in Table 4. It shows that the atomic percentages of T-CD and N-T-CD did not deviate significantly, but the CD has a much higher percentage of O (29.7 % vs. 12 %) and a very small fraction of N (3.7 % vs. 15 %) compared to the other two. The higher percentage of O in the CD is ascribed to the higher intensity of the O-H and C-O groups, as shown in the FTIR spectra. This result also implies that the CD has a higher degree of surface oxidation, resulting in a longer emitting wavelength of the PL spectrum [35,56].

The detail scanning spectra of C1 s and N1 s are shown in Fig. 7. It was found that the C 1 s peaks can be de-convoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 284.7 (C-C/CC), 285.4 (C-N) and 286.3 (C-O), respectively; the fractions of the three peaks are shown in Table 4. It is found that the T-CD and N-T-CD have similar percentages of the three bonding states. For the CDs, the percentage of C-C/CC is higher, and of the C-N and C-O they are smaller than the values of the other two CDs. For the N1 s spectra, four peaks at 398.5 eV (pyridinic Ns), 399.2 eV (amino N), 400.1 (pyrrolic Ns) and 401 eV (graphitic N) can be obtained by de-convolution [15,20,31,35]. It is known that pyridinic nitrogen is caused by substituting a carbon atom by nitrogen on the edges or defect sites in the plane, and pyrrolic nitrogen species usually refer to the exposing of nitrogen on planar edges or defect. For the three CDs, the percentages of amino N are similar (ca. 30 %), and most of the N atoms are presented as pyridinic state, especially in the T-CDs (64.5 %) and N-T-CDs (57.8 %). Regarding the pyrrolic N, CD has much higher percentage (21.9 %) than those of T-CD (4.8 %) and N-T-CD (4.0 %). In comparison to the CD, T-CD and N-T-CD have higher percentages of pyridinic and lower values of pyrrolic state.

Based on the data shown above, T-CD and N-T-CD have similar atomic percentages (Table 4), and close bonding states of C (Table 4) and N (Table 4), which is consistent with the similar PL properties of the two CDs. However, an apparent difference between T-CD and N-T-CD is the state of graphitic-N. Table 4 shows that the percentage of graphite-N of N-T-CD (9.6 %) is much higher than that of T-CD (1.9 %). It was reported that the graphitic-N in the CDs significantly affects the QYs [37]. The presence of graphitic-N may increase the rigidity of domains and hence reduce the non-radiative relaxation via molecular vibration on CD surfaces. Therefore, the higher QY of N-T-CD, compared to that of T-CD, may be ascribed to the higher percentage of graphite-N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model proposed for the growth of CDs on the TiO2 surface
ce:section.ce:para: The heterogeneous reaction of the precursors into CDs in the presence of TiO2 can be described by the classical nucleation and growth model [44,45]. Theoretically, the total Gibbs free energy forming a nucleus consists of volumetric (or bulk) free energy and surface free energy. Because the volumetric free energy is always negative, it means that the reaction proceeds spontaneously if only this energy is considered, whereas the surface energy being positive, it becomes an energy barrier to a reaction. Since the two energy terms have contrary effects on the reaction, there is a maximum free energy that should be overcome for the reaction to proceed; the nucleus size corresponding to the maximum free energy is the critical nucleus size (r * ), that is, the size at which a nucleus should pass through to form a stable nucleus.

When the precursor solution contains foreign particles, the reaction may occur on the surface of the particles, forming embryos on the particles surface. This heterogeneous nucleation is more significant when the embryo has high affinity to the particles surface. Usually, the affinity of an embryo is expressed by its contact angle on the support. The higher the affinity of the nucleus with the support, the smaller the contact angle that will be obtained; therefore, the energy barrier to the nucleation process will be decreased.

The nucleation and growth theory described above refers usually to a process of phase change without chemical reaction, e.g., condensation of vapor into liquid or solid phase. For the CD synthesis consisting of a chemical reaction, it is more complicated. The reaction of the oPD molecules may be carried out in the bulk phase, forming embryos and then adsorbing on the TiO2 surface. It is also possible that the oPD molecules adsorb first on the TiO2 surface and then undergo the reaction on the surface. In this case, the adsorbed oPD may react with other molecules on the surface or in the bulk. Of note, the reaction mechanism of the oPD adsorbed on the TiO2 surface may differ from that between molecules in the bulk (i.e., homogeneous reaction), thereby affecting the reaction rate, energy barrier and composition of the CDs. In this study, the reaction of heterogeneous can be carried out at a lower temperature (120 °C) and achieve an even higher QY, implying that the energy barrier is significantly decreased and the properties of the CDs differ from those prepared by the homogeneous process.

In conclusion, the CDs synthesized by the heterogeneous processes demonstrate obviously different properties in comparison to those prepared by homogeneous methods. By using 25 nm TiO2 nanoparticles as nucleation seeds of the heterogeneous system, smaller and multiple band gaps of the T-CDs were obtained, deduced from the red-shifts of the n-π * transition peak of the UV–vis absorbance spectra. Furthermore, the emission wavelengths of T-CDs blue shift from 530 nm (CDs) to 496 nm, and the QYs increased significantly from 18 % (CDs) to 68 % (T-CDs). Both properties of T-CDs and CDs have little dependence on the operation conditions. However, the QYs can increase further to a value as high as 85.9 % by employing N-doped TiO2 nanoparticles as the nucleation seeds. It was also found that the T-CDs prepared using 100 nm TiO2 particles have properties similar to those of CDs, indicating that particle size is an important factor in the heterogeneous process. The XPS analysis demonstrated that the heterogeneous reaction process resulted in higher percentages of C and N atoms, but lower fractions of O and pyrrolic N state of the T-CDs and N-T-CDs in comparison to those of the CDs, which are inferred to be the causes leading to their higher QYs and shorter emission wavelength.

Chung-Yao Chang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Andy Herman: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Shanmuganathan Venkatesan: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Chi-Lo Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Hsisheng Teng: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Yuh-Lang Lee: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan through grand MOST 108-2221-E-006-158-MY3 is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.168654.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on sustainable synthetic approaches toward photoluminescent quantum dots
